narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your Year 1 school decile of 5—roughly average among past students—and your Year 3 decile of 3, which is below the typical 5.5 average. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of similar students, estimated an 86% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk is why your application was rejected: law schools use this tool to prioritize applicants likely to pass the bar and succeed in practice. The three biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were, first and foremost, your LSAT score of 24—well below the average of about 37 seen in students who typically pass. Second, your Year 3 decile ranking played a key role in pushing toward a failure prediction, as lower performance there signals challenges in sustaining academic momentum compared to peers. Third, your undergraduate GPA of 2.4, lower than the 3.2 average, further nudged the model toward expecting bar struggles, though less strongly than the others. While your full-time status aligns with most successful students (over 90%), it wasn't enough to offset these core academic signals. In contrast to typical admitted students, who often have stronger test scores and consistent grade deciles, your metrics placed you in a higher-risk group for bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar circumstances thrive with targeted preparation—but the model weighs these predictors heavily based on historical outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework, and seeking bar prep resources early, like practice exams or tutoring tailored for underrepresented students. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools that value resilience like yours. Many have turned similar predictions around through focused effort.
